Really the only two big things Ghost in the Shell and Battle Angel Alita have in common is being Cyberpunk and a world were the majority of people are Cyborgs in some way. Other than that they have entirely different themes and narratives.

@Pravisamour I'd say the only major difference is why the heroines are fighting other cyborgs. Both have women, who died, and were saved by a scientist putting their minds into cyborgs, and then looked after by an older man who empathizes with them. And then their purpose becomes fighting other cyborgs.

Both want to understand more about where they came from and the hidden conspiracy in their world.

I understand this is an adaptation, and the movie hasn't been released yet, but I'm worried it won't be faithful to the manga.

here's the list of differences I think I spotted.
-Vector was never a full on villain in the manga, he was just a morally grey black market dealer who controlled supply lines to the scrapyard and Tiphares/Zalem. He was integral to Hugo's character development, but after that he became a reoccurring character.
-The big guy with the saw blade fingers was a villain that acted independently, and was never connected to Vector.
-The character Chiren is not a character in the manga, she's from the non-cannon 1993 OVA
-The woman with the blade hands also is not from the manga
-Zapan is also not associated with Vecter, he's a lone wolf bounty hunter (called Hunter Warriors in the story) that comes into conflict with Alita/Gally due to them pursuing the same target. (can't say anything more because there's spoilers).
-Ido found and was holding onto the berserker body long before Alita/Gally showed up. He knew exactly what it was and knew it was dangerous.
-In the manga, Alita/Gally destroys her "first" body when facing off against Makaku (saw blade fingers) for the first time, and she goes to the bar to ask the hunter warriors for help (which is also when Zapan is introduced) , and by then she has the berserker body. It doesn't seem like this is the case in the movie.

The manga is one of my all time favorites (I read all of Gunnm, Gunnm Last Order, and what was so far released for Gunnm Mars Chronicle in three days), and I wouldn't have found out about it were it not for the movie adaptation. This is an adaptation and I do believe adaptations should be able to stand on their own, no matter how close to the source material they are, I just hope that the movie isn't hurt by these story differences.

I am a massive fan of this manga as well. Most of the differences you pointed out don't concern me. The "blade woman" is probably the first cyborg Alita fights. It's not a big deal if she looks different from the manga. I don't see Zapan interacting with Vector at all in the trailer, so I don't know where you saw that. And if they want to expand the roles of minor characters like Vector for the movie, that could actually be interesting. 

You left out the one major difference that does bother me though and that's that Ido doesn't have the mark on his forehead. That's pretty important, if you know the story.

@PSYCHOBEVO 

Man, I completely forgot about the mark. And yeah, I don’t remember where I saw the zapan and vector thing. I probably made it up in the moment and didn’t realize it. I hope this will be a good movie in the end, whether it’s faithful or not.

She really does look like a full anime character plonked right in the middle of a live action film and its very jarring.

Getting a bit fed up of outlets covering this movie by saying that the fans of the original manga are "outraged about the size of her eyes". Im willing to bet my life the ones complaining have not only never read an original manga but have probably only got Dragonball or something similar as a reference. James Cameron decided to keep her design inkeeping with the original manga because he is a fan. Im a fan of the 80s & 90s manga because its what i grew up with and i think the look of Alita is amazing. Notice most human characters have augmented theyre bodies in some way but theyre heads are normal. This is how its represented in the original artwork. Yet Alita has these exaggerated eyes! This is because she is completely artificial except one internal organ her brain! So her whole body including her skull is artificial. Its not that hard a concept to grasp is it?

I've read the source material multiple times, her eyes are completely out of place and unjustifiable.
I can also point you towards other avid readers of the manga who feel the same way.

I find it hilarious how you deride those critical of this movie yet it looks like you haven't even read the manga yourself if you think all the other characters besides Alita should have normal heads. Zapan, Makaku and many more characters/extras have abnormal heads or exaggerated features.
Artstyle isn't meant to be taken literally.

The most obvious complaint someone would have with this is the main character has to be the one that has enormous eyes and a completely terribly looking animated face, the only one.

The whole trailer seemed to be stroking off the idea of how cool and rockin' this character is, showin' off all these sick tricks n' stunts yo. Honestly, makes me believe there's no compelling story to be shown, no characters to choose your favorites from.

Two concerns about this movie:
<1> It should never be acquired by Disney; Disney is great and I love Disney, but Gunnm/Alita and its sequels should be always rated R, and go as deep as its original manga does, otherwise it is just another superhero movie.
<2> Some people already told me they found it but I still can't see the mark of Salem on Dr. Ido's forehead -- I wish there is no plot change.

Asmobia Was the movie Elysium based on Battle Angel?

If a person tries to squeeze Battle Angel's story into 110 minutes instead doing a popular trilogy or tetralogy; if a producer removes the progress of searching/learning "human(?) nature", instead presenting a "good fighting against bad" story, then it really looks like Elysium.

Asmobia To be fair. Ive read Battle Angel Alita. The nine volume original series.  As far as searching and learning human nature that doesnt happen at all in the book. As soon as Ido builds her shes fighting as a bounty hunter. Sure she questions her past a few times but it feels like an afterthought. The series is mostly Alita fighting cyborgs and fighting mad scientist Desty Nova. Book two with the Yugo arc is the only part hat has an emotinal story.

It is a long story, it doesn't immediately run into the topic. The searching/learning process is slow, confusing, sometimes halt or even go side-ways. The plot is not a good guy whose destiny is to do right things so he/she did it by defeating all enemies in the path -- even there is a path, it should not be straight; that is why I like Alita, and I am afraid that Disney will make it a straight forward story which provides audience simple easy answer in 90 minutes.

In addition, the story is not only about Alita; many characters search the meaning of their lives. Some had happy ending, some ended tragedy, some turned mad and some just quit.

Asmobia The original series is really just Alita being a badass. She goes after Desty Nova only because he knows where Ido is. Not because she wants to save world. When Zappan talks about why he is doing the things he does and  his pain. Alita calls him a coward. The other characters arent searching for anything .They are just going about their day. When the motorball arc is over the question is where is Shumira? Oh she just got married . Not to Ido? No. Oh I guess that setup during volume 3 was for nothing. When Alita finds Ido after searching for him for multiple volumes where is he? Oh he freaked out about finding a chip in his brain . Wiped his memory and is now just back to being a doctor. Figure Four is introduced as Love Interest. Does he do anything noteworthy in the plot? No he just tags along with Alita then she leaves him to fight Desty Nova. I enjoyed the series but its just an action story. I dont get where your finding deep meaning in the manga. It's no deeper than any other action manga/anime from the 90's.

The majority of the story happens in the US.

"The story takes place in the former United States. According to a map printed in Fallen Angel Scrapyard and Tiphares are near Kansas City, Missouri, and the Necropolis is Colorado Springs, Colorado. Radio KAOS is at Dallas, Texas. Figure's coastal hometown is Alhambra, California. Desty Nova's Granite Inn is built out of a former military base - NORAD at Cheyenne Mountain, Colorado.[1]

Battle Angel Alita is revealed to take place in the 26th century. About midway through Last Order reference is made to a revised calendar known as Era Sputnik (ES), based on the launch of Sputnik 1 in 1957. A detailed timeline was later included with Gunnm: Another Stories."

just because those aren't the moves she makes in the manga. It just seemed weird for me to see that

To be honest I don't remember much of the specific moves in the manga, I read it quite some time ago.
What are the Kung Fu moves in the trailer? I think there's this flying kick, and this two punches at the same time move. Are there others I missed?
I remember Panzer Kunst was optimized for low-G and zero-G fighting. So when people fly around in over-the-top Kung Fu movies, I understand it's kind of ridiculous, but in low G it would make sense. Even here on Earth, Gally could pull it off, as a cyborg.
I also remember she was very mobile in the manga, jumping around, and even borderline flying (she could use plasma to propel herself if I remember correctly).
So to me it makes sense that when trying to translate the manga into a movie, they would take inspiration from Kung Fu movies to give that impression of over-the-top mobility and speed.

Well so far it looks and sounds EXACTLY like Ghost in the Shell

you're imagination is limited.. there are very few similarities.. but androids/cyborgs have been a central focus to japanese anime for 50+ years. Thousands of android & cyborg characters spanning across thousands of artist. Ghost in the shell and Battle Angel Alita are NOTHING alike... outside of both being cyborgs.

Vim Vitae Ghost in the Shell focused on the political and social aspects of a world where cyberization is integrated into a developed Society so much so that it makes people question the nature of the soul.

Battle Angel Alita on the other hand focused on a society where technology destroyed the world and how one's memories, both new and old, effect one's actions regardless of the state of society.

Overall Battle Angel Alita is a much more simplistic story that is more about the characters in it then Society itself. Which is why the result will probably end up being much better overall.

Most of the people I met have trouble understanding the political concepts behind Ghost in the Shell let alone the philosophical ones. So it's no surprise they rewrote the premise of the live-action version of Ghost in the Shell. Which was a mistake.

I know people are going to gripe about the changes, but really some things that work in comics dont float so well in movies. Usually when producing a largely visual spectacle action flick, they need to simplify the emotional and intellectual aspects to keep the tone consistent. It's going to be weird for me if Alita doesnt crack one liners under her breath, but it would probabaly worse if she did. Action movies often cant allow characters to grow in too many directions. It exhausts the audience and muddles the character objectives. I just hope that they keep her characterization consistent with the manga. Alita as a person does not continually fall back on male characters during her growth. She doesnt "need" anyone to continue to move forward. It's a very feminist theme that I hope translates well in the film.
TLDR: have problems, still will see.

Actually it was the other way around... Avatar was the tech demo to develop the full body mocap needed for Alita.
NameHierEinfuegen You're wrong guy..The other guy is right lol

Nope. I followed the development of this since Cameron got the rights around the year 2000. Dark Angel was his market study, to see if a story like that would be interesting for the audience. And Avatar was planned as "tech demo" to push cg and mocap boundaries and to see how full body mocap protagonists would resonate with the audience ... All in preparation for this. Sadly Avatar blew up to epic proportions and thanks to his contract he was then stuck directing the sequels instead of his dream project Alita.

NameHierEnfuegen is right.  Alita has been Cameron's passion project for decades, and part of Avatar's existence was to progress the technology for Alita.  Avatar's success prevented Cameron from working on Alita, which is why Rodriguez is directing instead.

Kentama "In 1994,[15] director James Cameron wrote an 80-page treatment for Avatar":- Wikipedia.

He waited for 11 years to get the hands on the technology required and took 4 years to create the masterpiece *_Avatar._* 

And now he is taking it to another level with Alita: Battle Angel to establish the foundation for Avatar quadrology.

It looks like PG-13 attraction with infuriatingly lame re-design of everything cyber, instead of what should be a heavily R-rated gritty vision of a cold terrible and immensely cynical dystopia, filled with godly tech design genius of Yukito Kishiro. Thus, the setting is off, most of the characters are off... This is Hollywood. Nobody is going to risk doing something new or picking up some uncomfortable themes. They will probably stick to the most PG adaptable story of Hugo and add some emasculated version of Makaku as seen in the trailer, plus about as much emasculated version of Motorball (what's left to it without cool-looking cyborgs and it being the violent sport?). Everything special is changed to safe and cliche - what is good about it? Just remember GitS film adaptation....
